description: Purge the Vite starter template. Delete App.css and starter assets. Configure Tailwind v4 with a Bloomberg-terminal dark-mode design system: true black backgrounds (zinc-950), muted white text (zinc-100), neon green (#00ff00) for profits/price drops, blood red (#ff0044) for losses/price increases, neutral muted zinc for idle UI. Tremor styles imported via index.css.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Tremor v3 ships its own component styles but relies on Tailwind for layout utilities. With Tailwind v4 via @tailwindcss/vite, there is no tailwind.config file — all customization goes through @theme in index.css. The dark theme is the ONLY theme (no light mode toggle). The neon-on-black palette creates a data-terminal aesthetic that signals 'this is a serious tool'.
implementation: 1. Delete src/App.css and src/assets/react.svg, src/assets/vite.svg (starter artifacts)
2. In src/index.css, after @import 'tailwindcss', add @theme block defining --color-neon-green: #00ff00, --color-blood-red: #ff0044, --color-surface-*: zinc-900/950, --color-text-*: zinc-100/400
3. Set html/body base styles: bg-zinc-950, text-zinc-100, font-family Inter/system-ui, antialiased
4. Import Tremor CSS for component styles
5. Add Vite proxy config in vite.config.ts: server.proxy '/api' → 'http://localhost:8000' to avoid CORS during dev
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: The Bloomberg-terminal aesthetic (neon on black) is the gold standard for financial/arbitrage dashboards. It communicates that this is an internal power tool, not a consumer storefront. CSS custom properties allow all components to reference the palette without hardcoding hex values.
architecture_proposal.cleanup: Delete App.css. Remove unused starter assets (react.svg, vite.svg). Keep hero.png.

description: Create the Zustand store that manages all ETL state: the last report (with delta), loading state, error state, and a strict 20-second cooldown timer. The store exposes a single triggerEtl() action that POSTs to /run-etl, manages the cooldown countdown, and stores the response. All components subscribe to slices of this store.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Zustand v5 supports the subscribeWithSelector middleware for granular re-renders. The cooldown timer uses setInterval inside the action — when triggerEtl() fires, it sets cooldownSeconds=20 and decrements every second via setInterval until 0. The store holds the FULL report JSON from POST /run-etl, including report.delta (price_drops, new_items, removed_items, price_increases, summary) and report.stages (scrape_catalog, category_structuring, storage with timing data).
implementation: 1. Define TypeScript interfaces: EtlReport (matching the exact backend JSON shape), DeltaSummary, PriceDrop, NewItem, RemovedItem, PriceIncrease
2. State shape: { report: EtlReport | null, isLoading: boolean, error: string | null, cooldownSeconds: number }

3. Action triggerEtl(demo?: boolean): if cooldownSeconds > 0 return early. Set isLoading=true. POST /api/run-etl?demo={demo}. On success: set report, clear error, start 20s countdown interval. On failure: set error string. Finally: set isLoading=false
4. The cooldown interval: setInterval(() => set(s => ({cooldownSeconds: s.cooldownSeconds - 1})), 1000). Clear interval when cooldownSeconds hits 0
5. Export selector hooks: useReport(), useDelta(), useIsLoading(), useCooldown() for component subscriptions
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Single source of truth. Every component reads from the same store — no prop drilling, no context providers. The cooldown is enforced in the store action itself so it cannot be bypassed by any UI component. Zustand's minimal API means the entire store is ~60 lines.

zombie_protection: fetch() with AbortController: if the request exceeds 90s, abort and set error='Pipeline timed out'. The backend has its own 120s timeout, but the frontend should fail slightly earlier to show a user-friendly message.

description: The 'Update Market Data' trigger button. Three visual states: idle (muted zinc, ready to click), loading (pulsing neon-green border + spinner + 'Scraping...'), cooldown (countdown timer, dimmed, disabled). Custom-styled — NOT Tremor Button — to match the terminal aesthetic with a neon-green glow on hover.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: The button subscribes to useEtlStore for isLoading, cooldownSeconds, and error. During loading, it shows an animated spinner icon and the text 'Scraping Costco...'. During cooldown, it shows 'Wait {N}s' with a muted appearance. On error, a blood-red error badge appears below. Idle state has a subtle neon-green border that glows on hover (box-shadow: 0 0 12px #00ff00).
implementation: 1. Import { useEtlStore } from the store
2. Subscribe to isLoading, cooldownSeconds, error, triggerEtl from the store
3. Render custom button with onClick={() => triggerEtl(true)}
4. Dynamic label: idle -> 'Update Market Data', loading -> 'Scraping Costco...', cooldown -> `Wait ${cooldownSeconds}s`
5. Loading state: add a spinning ArrowPathIcon (from @heroicons/react) before the label
6. Error display: conditional blood-red text below button showing last error message
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Custom styling over Tremor Button because the terminal aesthetic demands a specific neon-glow effect that Tremor's component API doesn't expose. The three-state pattern prevents double-clicks and VPS abuse.

zombie_protection: Button is forcefully disabled via the disabled prop when isLoading || cooldownSeconds > 0. Even if the store action has a guard, the UI enforces it visually.

description: A horizontal row of metric cards on dark zinc-900 surfaces with neon-green/blood-red value text. Cards: Total Products, Price Drops (neon-green), New Items, Removed (blood-red), Scrape Duration. Each card uses the terminal palette — no Tremor Card colors, pure dark surface with monospaced values.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Tremor's Card + Metric components render a KPI with a title, value, and optional delta badge. The delta.summary object maps 1:1 to the cards. When report is null (no run yet), cards show '--' placeholder values with muted zinc-600 text. Values use tabular-nums font-feature for aligned digits.
implementation: 1. Subscribe to report from useEtlStore
2. Extract delta.summary and stages.scrape_catalog.duration_s from report
3. Render a responsive grid (grid-cols-2 md:grid-cols-5) of dark-surface cards
4. Each card: muted zinc-400 title, large neon-green or blood-red value, monospace digits
5. Null state: render cards with '--' values and opacity-50 to indicate 'awaiting first run'
6. Animate value changes with a brief CSS transition on the metric text
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Metric cards are the universal dashboard pattern for at-a-glance KPIs. Neon-green for positive signals (price drops = opportunity), blood-red for negative signals (items removed = supply risk). Five cards give an instant pulse of the entire ETL run without scrolling.

zombie_protection: Defensive optional chaining on all report?.delta?.summary?.* accesses. If the report shape is unexpected, cards show '--' rather than crashing.

description: The core value proposition — a sortable data table consuming the price_drops array from the delta report. Neon-green savings badges, blood-red loss badges. Dark striped rows (alternating zinc-950/zinc-900). This is the table a hiring manager will stare at — it must look like a Bloomberg terminal.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: The price_drops array from compute_delta() contains: {id, name, old_price, new_price, delta, delta_pct}. The table renders this directly with Tremor's Table components. Savings are shown as neon-green badges (e.g. '-$10.00 / -33.4%'). Price increases shown as blood-red. Empty state: muted text suggesting an update run. Collapsible price_increases section below.
implementation: 1. Subscribe to report.delta.price_drops and report.delta.price_increases from useEtlStore
2. Render Tremor Table with columns: Product, Old Price, New Price, Savings ($), Savings (%), ID
3. Format prices with Intl.NumberFormat('en-US', {style: 'currency', currency: 'USD'})
4. Savings column: neon-green Badge for drops, blood-red Badge for increases
5. Empty state: centered muted text with ArrowPathIcon suggesting an update run
6. Add column header click to toggle sort direction (client-side sort on any column)
7. Collapsible 'Price Increases' section below with same table format but blood-red themed
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Dark striped rows with neon accents create the terminal-grade density needed for scanning large datasets. The badge-per-row pattern draws the eye to the largest arbitrage opportunities instantly. Client-side sorting is sufficient since the dataset is already in memory.

zombie_protection: price_drops defaults to [] if null/undefined. Table renders gracefully with empty body and a 'no data' message. Truncate product names at 80 chars with title tooltip for full text.

description: Assemble the final dashboard layout. Delete the entire Vite starter template from App.tsx. Build a single-page terminal layout: fixed top bar (zinc-900 border-b border-zinc-800) with 'COSTCO ARBITRAGE' in monospace + UpdateButton, MetricsBar below, ArbitrageTable as main content. Full black background. Update index.html title.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Single-page dashboard layout. No routing needed. The top bar is a flex row with the title in uppercase monospace (tracking-widest) on the left and the UpdateButton on the right. Below it, the MetricsBar spans full width. The ArbitrageTable fills the remaining viewport. A subtle footer shows 'Last updated: {report.finished_at}' in zinc-600.
implementation: 1. Delete all starter template JSX from App.tsx
2. Top bar: sticky, bg-zinc-900/80 backdrop-blur, border-b border-zinc-800, flex row — left: 'COSTCO ARBITRAGE' monospace, right: <UpdateButton />
3. Main content: max-w-7xl mx-auto px-4 — <MetricsBar /> on top, <ArbitrageTable /> below
4. Footer bar: zinc-600 text with last run timestamp, formatted via Intl.DateTimeFormat
5. Update index.html: <title>Costco Arbitrage</title>
6. Add responsive padding: on mobile the table scrolls horizontally, metrics stack 2-col
7. Add 'New Items' and 'Removed Items' summary sections between MetricsBar and ArbitrageTable
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: The terminal layout (sticky header, full-bleed dark background, monospace title) signals 'power tool'. max-w-7xl constrains readability on ultrawide monitors. The vertical flow (action -> KPIs -> data) mirrors how an operator processes information: trigger, assess, investigate.
architecture_proposal.cleanup: Delete App.css import from App.tsx. Remove all Vite starter content.

zombie_protection: Entire app wrapped in an ErrorBoundary component that catches render errors and shows a fallback UI with the error message instead of a white screen.
